votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks the length equality and verifies that each element in the result is the maximum of the corresponding prefix of the input list, exactly matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking the length equality and verifying that each element in the result is the maximum of the corresponding prefix.

The Python code correctly checks the length equality and iterates through the list to ensure that each element in the result is the maximum of the prefix up to that index, exactly matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks that result has the same length as numbers, and for each index i, it verifies that result[i] is in the prefix numbers[:i+1] and is greater than or equal to all elements in that prefix. The Coq spec uses firstn (S i) which gives the first i+1 elements, matching Python's numbers[:i+1]. The conditions In current_max prefix and Forall (fun x => x <= current_max) prefix are correctly translated to membership check and comparison loop.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks that result has the same length as numbers, and for each index i, it verifies that result[i] is in the prefix numbers[:i+1] and is greater than or equal to all elements in that prefix. The Coq spec uses 'In current_max prefix' and 'Forall (fun x => x <= current_max) prefix' which are exactly what the Python code checks with 'current_max not in prefix' and the inner loop checking 'x > current_max'.

Checks same length, membership of result[i] in prefix numbers[:i+1], and that no prefix element exceeds result[i], matching Coq’s per-prefix max condition.
